threats.title: Complete Protocol Takeover via Broken Access Control Modifiers
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyOwner and onlyManager modifiers evaluate boolean expressions instead of reverting, allowing any address to execute administrative functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Modifiers in Security.sol lack require() or revert() statements, rendering all access control checks non-functional and exposing all privileged operations to the public.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.sender == owner; instead of require(msg.sender == owner);

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Modifiers perform no state changes or reverts, acting as no-ops.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: All admin functions rely on these broken modifiers.

operational_questions: Can an attacker immediately call transferOwnership?
Can an attacker change fee destinations to drain protocol revenue?
validation_requirements: Test calling onlyOwner functions from a non-owner account.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker calls transferOwnership to gain owner role (optional, as they can just call functions directly).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls setProtocolFeePercent and setFeeRedistributor to redirect all fees to their own addresses.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls setERC20Factory to deploy malicious ERC20 tokens.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total compromise of fee routing and token deployment.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible if attacker transfers ownership and locks out legitimate admins.

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct calls to admin functions from EOAs.

threats.title: FeeSplitter Drain via Unprotected setCurves Function
threats.hypothesis_description: The setCurves function lacks access control, allowing any user to point the FeeSplitter to a malicious contract and manipulate fee claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: Without access control, an attacker can replace the trusted Curves contract reference with a malicious one that returns fabricated balances, enabling the theft of all ETH in the FeeSplitter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing access control modifier on state-changing configuration function.

preconditions: FeeSplitter contract holds accumulated ETH fees.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical dependency address can be changed by anyone.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Relies on Curves contract for balance and supply data.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever FeeSplitter has a non-zero ETH balance.

operational_questions: Can setCurves be called by an unprivileged user?
Does FeeSplitter rely entirely on the Curves contract for claim calculations?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock Curves contract returning inflated balances.
Call setCurves and attempt to drain FeeSplitter.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deploys a malicious contract mimicking Curves.sol that returns massive balances for the attacker.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls FeeSplitter.setCurves() with the malicious contract address.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls claimFees(), which uses the inflated balances to calculate a massive payout.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker drains all ETH from the FeeSplitter.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete theft of all user holder fees.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen ETH cannot be recovered.

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Ability to deploy a mock contract

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: FeeSplitter drained in a single transaction.

threats.title: Fee Theft via Uninitialized Receiver Offset on Transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: Token transfers do not update the receiver's fee offset, allowing attackers to claim historical fees they did not earn.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _transfer function in Curves.sol does not call feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange for the recipient. The recipient's userFeeOffset remains 0, allowing them to claim fees from the beginning of the token's lifetime.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Balance mapping updated without calling onBalanceChange for the recipient.

preconditions: Attacker has or can acquire tokens.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State synchronization missing between token balances and fee tracking.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after fees have accumulated.

operational_questions: Does a newly transferred token grant the receiver retroactive fee claims?
Can this be used to drain the FeeSplitter iteratively?
validation_requirements: Transfer tokens to a fresh address.
Check claimable fees for the fresh address.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker acquires tokens for a subject with high cumulative fees.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker transfers tokens to a new address they control.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The new address has a userFeeOffset of 0 but a positive balance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker calls claimFees from the new address, extracting fees calculated from offset 0.

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen ETH cannot be recovered.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Fee claiming for all legitimate users.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transfers followed immediately by large fee claims.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: FeeSplitter balance dropping below total legitimate claimable amounts.

threats.title: Destruction of Unclaimed Fees on Balance Change
threats.hypothesis_description: The onBalanceChange function overwrites a user's fee offset without crediting their accrued fees, causing permanent loss of unclaimed fees upon any token purchase.
threats.investigation_rationale: When a user buys tokens, onBalanceChange is called, which sets userFeeOffset to cumulativeFeePerToken. Because it does not call updateFeeCredit first, any fees the user had accrued prior to the purchase are permanently erased.

proof_of_signal.pattern: data.userFeeOffset[account] = data.cumulativeFeePerToken; executed without updating unclaimedFees.

preconditions: User has accrued but unclaimed fees.
User executes a buy transaction (or an attacker calls onBalanceChange directly due to broken modifiers).

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State overwrite without preserving historical accrual.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a user with unclaimed fees buys more tokens.

operational_questions: Are unclaimed fees lost when a user buys more tokens?
Can an attacker maliciously call onBalanceChange to wipe a victim's fees?
validation_requirements: Accrue fees for a user.
Have the user buy 1 more token.
Verify claimable fees drop to 0.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies a victim with a large amount of unclaimed fees.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls onBalanceChange(token, victim) directly (possible due to broken onlyManager modifier).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's userFeeOffset is updated to the current cumulative fee.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's accrued fees are permanently destroyed, effectively trapping the ETH in the FeeSplitter.

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual calculation and compensation from treasury.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about disappearing fee balances.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targeted calls to onBalanceChange for high-value holders.

threats.title: Permanent Locking of Protocol Fees on Sells
threats.hypothesis_description: During token sales, the protocol fee is deducted from the seller's payout but is never transferred to the protocol destination, permanently locking the ETH in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: In _transferFees, when isBuy is false, the sellValue is calculated by subtracting all fees (including protocolFee) from the price. However, the protocolFee is never sent to the protocolFeeDestination during a sell, stranding the ETH in the Curves contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sellValue deducts protocolFee, but firstDestination is msg.sender and no separate transfer sends protocolFee to protocolFeeDestination.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Asymmetric fee routing between buys and sells.

operational_questions: Does the Curves contract balance increase unexpectedly after sells?
Is there any mechanism to withdraw stuck ETH from the Curves contract?
validation_requirements: Execute a sell transaction.
Check protocol fee destination balance.
Check Curves contract ETH balance.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Normal protocol operation occurs (users buy and sell).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: On every sell, the protocol fee portion of the ETH is retained by the Curves contract.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Because there is no admin withdrawal function for stuck ETH, the funds are permanently locked.

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without contract upgrade (which is not supported).

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Curves contract ETH balance growing larger than the sum of bonding curve collateral.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Protocol revenue lower than expected based on volume.

threats.title: Permanent Locking of Excess ETH on Buys
threats.hypothesis_description: The buyCurvesToken function accepts payments greater than the required price and fees but fails to refund the excess ETH, permanently locking user funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _buyCurvesToken function checks if msg.value >= price + totalFee. If a user or a smart contract wallet sends more ETH than exactly required (e.g., to account for slippage), the excess is not refunded and remains stuck in the Curves contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.value < price + totalFee) revert... followed by no refund logic for msg.value > price + totalFee.

preconditions: User sends msg.value > exact price + fees.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Missing standard refund pattern for exact-payment functions.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any buy transaction with excess ETH.

operational_questions: Do frontends or routers send excess ETH to handle price changes?
Are users permanently losing overpayments?
validation_requirements: Send 2 ETH for a 1 ETH purchase.
Verify 1 ETH is permanently locked in the contract.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User attempts to buy a token and sends extra ETH to ensure the transaction doesn't revert if the price increases.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The contract processes the buy using the exact price.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The remaining ETH is left in the contract's balance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No withdrawal function exists, resulting in permanent loss of user funds.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User funds lost, severe reputational damage.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Curves contract ETH balance growing unexpectedly.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Transactions with high msg.value relative to token price.

threats.title: Fee Theft via Withdraw/Deposit Cycle
threats.hypothesis_description: Users can withdraw internal tokens to ERC20s, wait for fees to accrue at a higher rate, and deposit back to retroactively claim disproportionate fees.
threats.investigation_rationale: Withdrawing tokens reduces the internal supply and the FeeSplitter's totalSupply, concentrating future fee distributions among fewer holders. Because withdraw/deposit do not update the user's fee offset, a user can withdraw, let fees accrue at the concentrated rate, deposit back, and claim fees as if they held the tokens internally the entire time.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw and deposit change internal balances without calling feeRedistributor.onBalanceChange.

preconditions: Token has active trading volume generating fees.
Attacker holds a significant portion of the token supply.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State changes affecting fee calculations bypass fee offset updates.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During periods of high trading volume.

operational_questions: Does withdrawing tokens accurately reflect in the FeeSplitter's total supply?
Can a user claim fees for periods when their tokens were wrapped as ERC20s?
validation_requirements: Withdraw tokens to ERC20.
Generate fees via other users trading.
Deposit tokens back.
Claim fees and verify the amount exceeds fair share.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker withdraws internal tokens to ERC20, reducing FeeSplitter totalSupply.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Other users trade, generating holder fees which are distributed at a higher per-token rate due to the reduced supply.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker deposits ERC20s back to internal tokens. Their userFeeOffset remains stale.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker calls claimFees, retroactively capturing the high-rate fees for their entire balance, draining the FeeSplitter.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of yields from legitimate holders.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen ETH cannot be recovered.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large withdraws followed by deposits and immediate fee claims.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Cyclical wrapping and unwrapping of tokens.

threats.title: Unauthorized ERC20 Deployment via Withdraw
threats.hypothesis_description: Any token holder can trigger the deployment of a subject's ERC20 token via the withdraw function, bypassing the subject's ability to set a custom name and symbol.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function calls _deployERC20 directly if the token hasn't been deployed yet. It does not use the onlyTokenSubject modifier. An attacker can buy 1 token, immediately withdraw, and force the ERC20 deployment with default metadata, locking out the subject.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw function lacks onlyTokenSubject modifier but initiates token deployment.

preconditions: Subject has not yet deployed their ERC20 token.
Subject has not yet set custom name/symbol.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Privileged operation (token deployment) accessible via unprivileged user workflow.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between token creation and subject's explicit mint/setName call.

operational_questions: Can a non-subject deploy the ERC20 token?
Does this permanently prevent the subject from setting a custom name?
validation_requirements: Buy a token as a non-subject.
Call withdraw.
Verify ERC20 is deployed with default name.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker monitors for new curve creations.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker buys 1 token of the new curve.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The ERC20 token is deployed with 'Curves' default name. The subject can never change it because setNameAndSymbol reverts if token is already deployed.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of branding and identity for token creators.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without deploying a new curve.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: withdraw called by non-subject immediately after curve creation.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Many tokens deployed with default names against creator wishes.

threats.title: Unbounded Array Bloat in FeeSplitter userTokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The onBalanceChange function pushes tokens to the userTokens array on every purchase without checking for duplicates, allowing attackers to bloat the array and cause out-of-gas errors.
threats.investigation_rationale: Every time a user buys a token, onBalanceChange pushes the token address to the userTokens array. Repeated buys of the same token will add duplicate entries, growing the array unbounded and eventually causing batchClaiming and getUserTokensAndClaimable to revert due to block gas limits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (balanceOf(token, account) > 0) userTokens[account].push(token); without duplicate check.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Array push operation without uniqueness validation.

operational_questions: Does userTokens accumulate duplicates?
How many buys does it take to exceed the block gas limit for batchClaiming?
validation_requirements: Buy the same token 10 times.
Check the length of userTokens array.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker identifies a target user (or targets themselves to grief the UI).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker repeatedly buys 0 tokens (or minimal amounts) of a token the victim holds. (Note: buyCurvesToken with 0 amount succeeds and triggers onBalanceChange).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The victim's userTokens array grows massively with duplicate entries.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: When the victim attempts to use the UI (which calls getUserTokensAndClaimable) or batchClaiming, the transaction runs out of gas.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users unable to claim fees or load UI.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - users must manually call claimFees for individual tokens instead of batch.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Out of gas errors on batchClaiming.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated interactions with the same token by the same user.

threats.title: Initial Purchase Underflow in getPrice
threats.hypothesis_description: The getPrice function reverts due to an arithmetic underflow when supply == 0 and amount > 1, forcing token subjects to buy exactly one token during initialization.
threats.investigation_rationale: In getPrice, the calculation for sum2 uses (supply - 1 + amount). In Solidity 0.8.7, arithmetic is checked. If supply is 0, (0 - 1) underflows and reverts before amount is added. This breaks buyCurvesTokenWithName and buyCurvesTokenForPresale if amount > 1.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Mathematical formula fails for valid edge case inputs.

operational_questions: Can a subject initialize a curve with multiple tokens?
Does this force subjects into a multi-step initialization?
validation_requirements: Call buyCurvesTokenWithName with amount = 5.
Verify transaction reverts with arithmetic underflow.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Subject attempts to create a curve and buy 10 tokens to establish initial liquidity.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Subject is forced to buy exactly 1 token to initialize.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Subject must send a second transaction to buy the remaining 9 tokens, creating operational friction and suboptimal user experience.

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - subject just buys 1 token first.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Subjects always buying exactly 1 token initially.

threats.title: Trading Halt via Zero Supply Revert in addFees
threats.hypothesis_description: If all circulating internal tokens for a subject are wrapped into ERC20s, the FeeSplitter's totalSupply becomes zero, causing addFees to revert and halting all trading for that token.
threats.investigation_rationale: FeeSplitter.totalSupply() calculates (internal supply - locked in contract). If all tokens are withdrawn to ERC20, this equals 0. During a buy/sell, _transferFees calls addFees, which reverts if totalSupply == 0, completely breaking the buy/sell functionality.
preconditions: All internal token holders withdraw their tokens to ERC20.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Critical trading path depends on external contract state that can legitimately reach zero.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When internal circulating supply reaches zero.

operational_questions: Does addFees revert when all tokens are wrapped?
Does this revert propagate to block buys and sells?
validation_requirements: Create a token, buy supply.
Withdraw 100% of supply to ERC20.
Attempt to buy or sell internal tokens.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker buys all available internal tokens (or coordinates with other holders).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls withdraw() for their entire balance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Any subsequent buy or sell attempts revert in _transferFees -> addFees, permanently halting the token's market until someone deposits ERC20s back.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of trading for the affected token.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - requires someone to deposit ERC20s back, but market is frozen until then.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: When internal supply is highly concentrated

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Total internal supply matching locked supply.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reverts on buy/sell with NoTokenHolders error.
